title: VA Math Explained: Why 50% + 30% Doesn't Equal 80%
excerpt: The combined ratings table confuses almost every veteran. Here's a plain-English guide to how VA calculates your overall disability percentage -- with real examples and strategic implications.
author: Chris Combs
summary: VA 'fuzzy math' uses a descending efficiency scale where each subsequent disability is a percentage of the remaining 'whole' person, not the original 100%. This guide explains the calculation formula and why reaching a 100% combined rating requires significantly more than a simple addition of percentages.
keyTakeaway: VA combined ratings use a descending efficiency formula -- each additional disability is a percentage of the remaining whole person, not a simple addition.
whyItMatters: Understanding VA math reveals why reaching 100% combined requires strategic planning. The difference between 90% and 100% can be tens of thousands of dollars annually.
whatThisMeans: Use the VA combined rating formula to calculate where you stand and identify which additional claims would push you to the next rating threshold. Every percentage point counts.
body:
Every veteran who has more than one service-connected disability hits the same wall. You get rated 50% for your back and 30% for your knee, you pull out a calculator, and you expect to see 80%. Instead, VA tells you your combined rating is 65%. You stare at the letter. You check the math again. You wonder if someone at the regional office failed third-grade arithmetic.

They didn't. VA uses a completely different system -- one laid out in 38 CFR 4.25 -- that treats your body as a whole rather than adding up individual percentages. It's called the 'combined ratings table,' and once you understand how it works, you can use it to your strategic advantage. But first, you have to accept that VA math is not regular math.

## :brain: Why VA Doesn't Use Simple Addition

The logic behind VA's system starts with a basic premise: your body is 100% of a whole person. Each disability rating represents the percentage of your 'whole person' that is disabled. When VA combines ratings, it doesn't stack percentages on top of each other -- it applies each successive rating to the remaining non-disabled portion of your body.

Think of it this way. If your back is rated 50%, VA considers 50% of you disabled and 50% of you still 'whole.' When they add your 30% knee rating, that 30% doesn't apply to the original 100%. It applies to the remaining 50% that's still considered non-disabled. So 30% of 50% is 15%. Add that to the original 50%, and you get 65% combined.

The formula is straightforward once you see it:

- Start with your highest rating
- Apply the next rating to the remaining non-disabled percentage
- Repeat for each additional rating
- Round the final result to the nearest 10%

That rounding step is where a lot of heartbreak -- or unexpected wins -- happens. But we'll get there.

> [!important]
> VA math always applies each new rating to the REMAINING non-disabled percentage, not to the original 100%. This is why two 50% ratings don't equal 100% -- they equal 75% combined (50% + 50% of the remaining 50%).

## :book: The Combined Ratings Table Under 38 CFR 4.25

38 CFR 4.25 contains the actual combined ratings table that VA raters use. You don't have to do the multiplication yourself -- the table does it for you. It's a grid where you find your first rating on one axis, your second rating on the other axis, and the intersection gives you the combined value.

Here's how to use it step by step:

### Step 1: Line Up Your Ratings from Highest to Lowest

Always start with your highest individual rating. This isn't just convention -- it's how the table is designed to work, and starting with the highest rating first ensures the most accurate combined value.

Say you have three ratings: 50%, 30%, and 20%.

### Step 2: Combine the First Two

Find 50 on one axis and 30 on the other. The table gives you 65. That's the combined value of your first two ratings.

Mathematically: 50 + 30(1 - 0.50) = 50 + 15 = 65.

### Step 3: Combine the Result with the Next Rating

Now take that 65 and combine it with your 20% rating. Find 65 on one axis and 20 on the other. The table gives you 72.

Mathematically: 65 + 20(1 - 0.65) = 65 + 7 = 72.

### Step 4: Round to the Nearest 10%

Your combined value is 72. Under 38 CFR 4.25(a), VA rounds to the nearest number divisible by 10. Anything ending in 5 or higher rounds up; anything ending in 4 or lower rounds down. So 72 rounds down to 70%.

Your final combined rating: 70%.

If that combined value had been 75 instead of 72, you'd round up to 80%. One diagnostic code, one percentage point, can mean the difference between two rating tiers and hundreds of dollars a month.

> [!tip]
> You can verify VA's math yourself. The formula for combining two ratings A and B is: Combined = A + B(1 - A/100). Always convert percentages to decimals for the multiplication, then work from highest to lowest.

## :chart: Real-World Examples That Matter

Let's walk through scenarios that hit common rating thresholds.

### Example 1: Chasing 100% with Multiple Ratings

A veteran has the following ratings: 70%, 30%, 20%, 10%.

- 70 + 30 = 79 (from table)
- 79 + 20 = 83
- 83 + 10 = 85
- Round 85 up to 90%

Even with four separate service-connected conditions, this veteran lands at 90% -- not 100%. To reach a schedular 100% combined rating, you typically need either a single 100% rating or a combination that pushes above 95% before rounding.

### Example 2: The Frustrating Near-Miss

A veteran has ratings of 40%, 20%, and 10%.

- 40 + 20 = 52
- 52 + 10 = 57
- Round 57 down to 60%

That 57 is brutal -- three percentage points short of rounding up to 60%. If the 10% had been 20% instead, the combined would be 62, which still rounds to 60%. But if that 40% had been 50%, the whole picture changes: 50 + 20 = 60, then 60 + 10 = 64, rounding down to 60%. The lesson: increasing a lower rating sometimes has zero impact on the combined total because of how rounding works.

### Example 3: Two Big Ratings

A veteran has 70% and 50%.

- 70 + 50 = 85
- Round 85 up to 90%

Notice how 70 + 50 in VA math equals 85, not 120. That 50% only applies to the remaining 30% of 'whole person,' giving you 15 points -- not 50.

> [!warning]
> Never assume that adding a new rating will bump you to the next tier. Always run the combined ratings calculation BEFORE filing to understand the actual impact. A 10% increase in a lower rating might produce zero change in your combined percentage after rounding.

## :shield: The Bilateral Factor Under 38 CFR 4.26

Here's where most guides stop, but we're going deeper. If you have disabilities affecting both arms, both legs, or paired skeletal muscles, you're entitled to the 'bilateral factor' under 38 CFR 4.26. This is a 10% bonus applied to the combined value of your bilateral disabilities before they get folded into the overall combined rating.

Here's how it works:

### How to Calculate the Bilateral Factor

1. Identify your bilateral disabilities (same type of disability affecting both sides -- e.g., 20% left knee and 10% right knee)
2. Combine those bilateral ratings using the combined ratings table (20 + 10 = 28)
3. Multiply that combined value by 10% (28 x 0.10 = 2.8)
4. Add that 10% bonus to the bilateral combined value (28 + 2.8 = 30.8)
5. Use 30.8 (not 28) when combining with your other ratings

The bilateral factor recognizes that having bilateral disabilities creates greater functional impairment than the same ratings on one side. A veteran with radiculopathy in both legs has a harder time walking than a veteran with the same total rating in one leg and an unrelated condition.

### When It Matters

The bilateral factor can push you over a rounding threshold. In our example, the bilateral combined went from 28 to 30.8. If the rest of your ratings put you at, say, 73 combined, that 2.8-point bump from the bilateral factor could push you to 75.8, which rounds to 80% instead of 70%. That's real money.

> [!tip]
> Common bilateral pairs that trigger 38 CFR 4.26: bilateral knee conditions, bilateral hearing loss with tinnitus affecting both ears, bilateral radiculopathy (lower or upper extremities), bilateral plantar fasciitis, bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome, and bilateral shoulder conditions. If you have any paired disability, make sure the bilateral factor was applied.

## :target: Strategic Implications -- Filing Order and Timing

Once you understand VA math, you can make smarter decisions about which claims to file and when. Here are the strategic takeaways.

### Highest Ratings Move the Needle Most

Because each successive rating applies to a shrinking 'remaining' percentage, higher individual ratings produce much larger jumps in the combined total than lower ones. Going from 0% to 50% on a single condition adds 50 points. But adding a 10% rating when you're already at 80% combined adds only 2 points (10% of the remaining 20%).

This means you should prioritize increasing your highest-rated conditions before chasing new 10% ratings. Getting a 50% rating increased to 70% will almost always move your combined rating more than adding three new 10% conditions.

### Know Your Rounding Threshold

Before filing a new claim, calculate where your combined rating will land AFTER rounding. If you're currently at a combined value of 72 (which rounds to 70%), you need to push past 75 to reach the 80% tier. A new 10% rating would bring you to approximately 75 -- right on the edge. A 20% rating would bring you to approximately 78, safely rounding to 80%.

### The 94.5% Rule

To reach a schedular 100% combined rating, your pre-rounding combined value must be 95% or higher (since 95 rounds up to 100, but 94 rounds down to 90%). Getting from 90% to 100% is the hardest gap to close because each new rating applies to just the remaining 10%. You'd need a single additional rating of 50% just to add 5 points at that level.

This is why many veterans pursue Total Disability based on Individual Unemployability (TDIU) under 38 CFR 4.16 rather than trying to reach schedular 100%. TDIU pays at the 100% rate if your service-connected disabilities prevent you from maintaining substantially gainful employment.

### Don't Ignore 0% Ratings

A 0% (noncompensable) service-connected rating establishes the connection. If that condition worsens later, you file for an increase -- not a brand-new claim. This also opens the door for secondary service connection claims. A 0% rated condition that later causes a new disability can still be the basis for a secondary claim under 38 CFR 3.310.

## :tools: Common Mistakes Veterans Make with VA Math

### Assuming Simple Addition

The most common mistake. Veterans see 50% + 30% and expect 80%. When they get 65%, they think VA made an error. It's not an error -- it's the combined ratings table working as designed under 38 CFR 4.25.

### Missing the Bilateral Factor

VA raters sometimes fail to apply the bilateral factor. If you have bilateral conditions and your rating decision doesn't mention 38 CFR 4.26, request a review. This is a clear and unmistakable error (CUE) if the bilateral factor should have been applied and wasn't.

### Not Challenging Individual Ratings

Because of how VA math works, the most effective way to increase your combined rating is often to increase an existing individual rating rather than adding new conditions. If your PTSD is rated at 50% but your symptoms support 70%, that single increase can move your combined rating by 10-15 points -- more than multiple new 10% conditions would add.

### Ignoring Rounding Math Before Filing

Filing a claim for a 10% condition when your combined value is 71% (rounds to 70%) will likely produce a combined value of about 74% -- which still rounds to 70%. You just went through a year-long claims process for zero additional compensation. Run the numbers first.

> [!warning]
> If you believe VA miscalculated your combined rating, check three things: (1) Did they apply the bilateral factor for paired disabilities? (2) Did they use the correct individual ratings? (3) Did they round correctly? If any of these are wrong, file for correction under 38 CFR 3.105(a) for clear and unmistakable error.

## :medal: The Bottom Line

VA math isn't broken -- it's just different. Under 38 CFR 4.25, each disability rating applies to the remaining non-disabled portion of your body, which is why combined ratings always come out lower than simple addition would suggest. The bilateral factor under 38 CFR 4.26 provides a 10% bump for paired disabilities. And rounding to the nearest 10% can make or break your rating tier.

The veterans who come out ahead are the ones who understand these mechanics before they file. Calculate your combined rating using the table. Identify whether the bilateral factor applies. Target the individual rating increases that will actually push you past a rounding threshold. And if VA's math doesn't match yours, challenge it -- because raters make mistakes too.

> [!check]
> Before your next claim, run this checklist: (1) Calculate your current combined value before rounding, (2) determine what the new rating would change that value to, (3) check if it crosses a rounding threshold, (4) verify the bilateral factor is applied if applicable, and (5) consider whether increasing an existing rating would be more effective than adding a new one. Math wins claims.
